title: Audio: Backtracking — walk the decision tree

cues.label: The trigger
cues.headline: "Find all the ways" needs a variable loop count
cues.narration: When the number of nested loops depends on the input, you cannot write them out; backtracking enumerates every choice sequence instead.

cues.label: A tree of choices
cues.headline: Every root-to-leaf path is one answer
cues.narration: Picture a tree where each branch is a choice; a depth-first walk visits every candidate answer.

cues.label: Three beats
cues.headline: Choose, explore, un-choose
cues.narration: Push a choice onto a shared path, recurse to explore, then pop it back off so the next branch starts clean.

cues.label: Record a copy
cues.headline: Snapshot at a complete state
cues.narration: At a complete answer, save a copy of the path, never the live list, or later pops corrupt every saved answer.

cues.label: Prune early
cues.headline: Delete doomed subtrees
cues.narration: Reject a partial state the instant you can prove it is hopeless; an early rejection erases the largest subtrees.

cues.label: Index or marker
cues.headline: Start index vs used-array
cues.narration: A start index prevents duplicate subsets; a used-array lets permutations place any free element in any order.

cues.label: When it fails
cues.headline: Exponential; DP may fold it
cues.narration: The tree is often exponential or factorial; if you need one optimum or a count, dynamic programming can fold it to a table.
